rulings.Case Title: JOHN LEE et al VS. MOLNIR FUND L.P. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Amend Application For And Renewal Of Judgment, Or Alternatively, Court Leave To File An Application For And Renewal Of Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 1. PLAINTIFFS JOHN LEE, FREDERIC CLARK, INDIVIDUALLY, AND AS TRUSTEE FOR THE CASILLAS FOUNDATION, AND KURT THIEL's Motion To Amend Application For And Renewal Of Judgment, Or Alternatively, Court Leave To File An Application For And Renewal Of Judgment. The unopposed motion is granted. Plaintiff is granted leave of the court to amend the Application for and Renewal of Judgment to reflect the full name of the Plaintiff as listed on the amended original judgment and in the complaint. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: L. B. VS. DOE 1, A CALIFORNIA NONPROFIT CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 6. 2 - DEFENDANT DOE 1's Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents, Set One. The court denies the motion to compel further responses to request for production of documents as untimely. In answers served on July 2, 2024, Plaintiff asserted, in verified responses, that she did not have any responsive documents. Defendant did not move to compel further responses within the 45 days provided. The discovery deadline to move to compel is jurisdictional, and the court lacks authority to order further responses at this point. (Weinstein v. Blumberg (2018) 25 Cal.App.5th 316, 322, fn. 3 ["Prejudice is not required; discovery deadlines are mandatory and we have treated them as jurisdictional (Sexton v. Superior Court (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1410), even though a trial court may grant relief from deadlines to file motions to compel. Where a party does not obtain trial court relief from the statutory deadline, 'failure to move for further answers within the statutory time forecloses further relief ....' (O'Brien v. Superior Court (1965) 233 Cal.App.2d 388, 391.)"].) Because Plaintiff's responses were straightforward and verified representations of her lack of documents, it is not clear what relief the court could order even if the motion were timely. Defendant has not prevailed and is not entitled to sanctions. Plaintiff would be entitled to sanctions because Defendant's position is without substantial justification, but Plaintiff has not sought sanctions. The court does not award sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: L. B. VS. DOE 1, A CALIFORNIA NONPROFIT CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of And Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories, Set Two
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 6. 1 - DEFENDANT DOE 1's Motion To Compel Further Response To Form Interrogatories, Set Two. The court grants the motion to compel further response to Form Interrogatories, Set Two, in part. The court does not find persuasive the argument that the court should use meet and confer letters to test the truthfulness of an interrogatory response. The fact that defendant believes additional documents or information should be available does not make it so. Plaintiff has verified her responses and is thereby bound to them. However, the court finds that Plaintiff's initial verified form interrogatory responses were not sufficiently complete or straightforward. Plaintiff has cured the deficiencies in part by straightforward, verified amended responses, served after the filing of the motion, that make clear she does not remember the names or locations of any treatment provider. But her initial verified responses stated that she lost her job as a security guard as a result of the alleged sexual abuse. On this account, her previous employer is a person with knowledge of her injuries. Plaintiff shall provide the name and contact information of this previous employer in an amended response due within 10 days of entry of this order. Defendant seeks sanctions. The court finds that Plaintiff's initial responses, and her failure to amend during meet and confer efforts, were not substantially justified. (See Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390, 411 (court may award sanctions even where a party amends while motion to compel is pending].) The court reduces the requested sanctions amount in light of Defendant's modest success. Plaintiff shall pay $100 in sanctions to Defendant through counsel within 10 days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. rulings.Case Title: XIZHEN ZHU ET AL VS. KINGDOM OF DUMPLING LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu To Arbitrate This Case, And Stay The Current Proceeding
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 4. DEFENDANT KINGDOM OF DUMPLING LLC's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Xizhen Zhu To Arbitrate This Case, And Stay The Current Proceeding. Defendant's motion to compel arbitration and stay the current proceedings is denied. Plaintiff contests the existence of an agreement to arbitrate. Even assuming there is an agreement to arbitrate, the court finds that the defendant has waived its right to compel arbitration. The benefit of an arbitration agreement may be waived by a party. "In determining waiver, a court can consider (1) whether the party's actions are inconsistent with the right to arbitrate; (2) whether the litigation machinery has been substantially invoked and the parties were well into preparation of a lawsuit before the party notified the opposing party of an intent to arbitrate; (3) whether a party either requested arbitration enforcement close to the trial date or delayed for a long period before seeking a stay; (4) whether a defendant seeking arbitration filed a counterclaim without asking for a stay of the proceedings; (5) whether important intervening steps [e.g., taking advantage of judicial discovery procedures not available in arbitration] had taken place; and (6) whether the delay affected, misled, or prejudiced the opposing party." (St. Agnes Medical Center v. PacifiCare of California (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1187, 1196 [internal quotation marks and citation omitted; brackets in original].) Here, Defendant answered the complaint on June 22, 2023 and did not assert that the court lacked jurisdiction because the parties had agreed to arbitrate. Defendant participated in litigation for over a year and invoked the authority of the court to obtain discovery from the Plaintiff in a recent motion to compel the Plaintiff's deposition. Trial is now set for March 17, 2025. At least the first three and possibly the fifth of the factors identified in St. Agnes are present. On these facts, the court finds that Defendant waived the benefit of the arbitration agreement, if it exists. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: STUDENT LOAN SOLUTIONS, LLC VS. CURTIS U COOPER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 8 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). CROSS COMPLAINANT CURTIS COOPER III's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories And Request For Production Of Documents. Defendant and cross-complainant Curtis Cooper III moves to compel further responses to his first set of requests for production of documents and special interrogatories from plaintiff and cross-defendant Student Loan Solutions, LLC (SLS). Cooper also seeks sanctions. For the reasons stated herein, the motion is granted in part and denied in part. Cooper's requests are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence except as discussed individually below. As discussed individually below, most of his requests are supported by good cause. SLS's objections, other than its privilege objections, are not well taken except as discussed individually below. SLS has not produced documents because, it alleges, Cooper has refused to sign or negotiate a protective order to prevent produced documents from being filed in public court filings. A party who is asked to produce documents that it believes are confidential or subject to privacy or trade-secrets protections may negotiate a protective order first, may assert those objections in its responses until a protective order is in place, or may move the court for a protective order. SLS elected the second option. Having done so, and Cooper having filed a motion to compel showing good cause for production, it was incumbent upon SLS in its opposition papers to demonstrate that its confidentiality objections have merit. (See Kirkland v. Superior Court (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 92, 98 ["Once good cause was shown, the burden shifted to [responding party] to justify his objection."].) SLS has not made that showing; it has only asserted SLS's good-faith belief in its need for a protective order, without stating why that belief is founded or why the documents are entitled to protection. The assertion alone is not enough; a party bears the burden of justifying its objections. Accordingly, the court overrules SLS's confidentiality objections. SLS contends that Cooper has not satisfied his meet-and-confer obligations. The court disagrees; SLS defended its interrogatory responses in its letter and Cooper disagreed. The dispute is straightforward enough that no further meet-and-confer was required. With respect to meet and confer efforts about a protective order, Cooper asked SLS for further information about the nature of documents to be kept confidential, and SLS did not provide a substantive response, instead merely asserting that it was Cooper's obligation to negotiate for a protective order even without an explanation for why documents were entitled to protection. Here, too, the court finds that Cooper's informal resolution efforts were adequate. With respect to individual responses: Except as to objections based on attorney-client privilege, the court overrules SLS's objections and finds its responses to be evasive and incomplete as to Special Interrogatory Nos. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8. The court sustains SLS's scope objections to Special Interrogatory Nos. 9 and 10. The fact of filing of other cases is not directly relevant to any party's claims or defenses. The fact that a party has won other cases or lost other cases is not admissible evidence and the requests are not likely to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Cooper has not shown good cause. The court does not overrule any attorney-client privilege objection. The court overrules remaining SLS's objections, including its confidentiality objection, as to RFP Nos. 1-4, 6, 7, 8-10. (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: STUDENT LOAN SOLUTIONS, LLC VS. CURTIS U COOPER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: CROSS COMPLAINANT CURTIS COOPER III'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES AND REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 8 (Part 2 of 2 of the tentative ruling). CROSS COMPLAINANT CURTIS COOPER III'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES AND REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS. With respect to RFP Nos. 5: A party seeking an order for further responses is required to show good cause, supported by specific facts. A declaration is not always necessary to provide those specific facts where the basis for demanding production is relevant, in this court's view, but the less apparent the relevance the greater explanation or justification is required. This request appears to call for information concerning Cooper's alleged loan and other loans purchased together on a particular date. Cooper has not carried his burden of showing good cause. SLS shall provide verified amended responses, and a privilege log reflecting any documents withheld pursuant to attorney-client privilege, within 20 days of entry of this order. Cooper also seeks sanctions. The court shall order sanctions against the party unsuccessfully opposing a discovery motion unless it finds that the opposing party acted with substantial justification or other circumstances make imposition of sanctions unjust. The court does not find substantial justification here but reduces Cooper's award to reflect his mixed success with this motion. The court orders SLS to pay $3,000 to Cooper in sanctions within 20 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (end of part 2 of 2 of the tentative ruling) =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES BALLARD VS. OPEN TOP SIGHTSEEING SAN FRANCISCO, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT'S DEMURRER TO AMENDED COMPLAINT (ADDED FOR POSTING OF PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 7 (Part 2 of 2 of the tentative ruling). DEFENDANT OPEN TOP SIGHTSEEING SAN FRANCISCO, LLC's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Similarly, the demurrer to the Labor Code section 1102.5 claim is sustained without leave to amend. Weingand, supra, 2012 WL 3537035 at *4 ["Plaintiff cannot make out a section 1102.5 claim because he does not allege that he reported any suspicions of unlawful activity to any government agency, nor does he allege that he refused to do anything that would violate the law. Rather, he merely asserts that he complained about Defendant's practices and questioned whether they were legal to his own human resources department. Such a complaint does not raise whistleblower protection under section 1102.5."].) The demurrer to the Labor Code section 6310 claim is overruled. California Labor Code section 6310 prohibits adverse employment actions because an employee has made "any oral or written complaint . . . to his or her employer . . . about unsafe working conditions or unsafe work practices." (Rodriguez v. Lab. Corp. of Am. (C.D. Cal. 2022) 623 F.Supp.3d 1047, 1056.) Paragraph 9 of the FAC sufficiently alleges complaints about safety and retaliation. rulings.Case Title: CHARLES BALLARD VS. OPEN TOP SIGHTSEEING SAN FRANCISCO, LLC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 5, 2025, Line 7 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) DEFENDANT OPEN TOP SIGHTSEEING SAN FRANCISCO, LLC's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Defendant's demurrer to the First Amended Complaint ("FAC") is sustained without leave to amend as to causes of action seven [Labor Code section 98.6] and eight [Labor Code section 1102.5] and overruled as to the remainder. (CCP 430.10(e).) Per CCP 425.10(a)(1), the complaint "shall contain[a] statement of the facts constituting the cause of action, in ordinary and concise language." The court liberally construes the allegations of the complaint. (CCP 452.) To establish a prima facie case of discrimination under FEHA, Plaintiff must show that "he or she was a member of a protected class; was qualified for the position he sought; suffered an adverse employment action, and there were circumstances suggesting that the employer acted with a discriminatory motive." (Jones v. Dep't of Corrections (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 1367, 1379). Contrary to defendant's argument, the complaint alleges sufficient ultimate facts that plaintiff was performing competently/qualified (FAC, par. 13) and circumstances suggesting a discriminatory motive (FAC, pars. 7-8, 14). No further evidentiary facts are required to be pleaded; discovery will enable defendant to learn further details. (See Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531, 550 ["the complaint ordinarily is sufficient if it alleges ultimate rather than evidentiary facts"].) To establish a prima case of retaliation under FEHA, Plaintiff must show (1) he or she engaged in protected activity; (2) the employer then subjected the employee to an adverse employment action; and (3) there is a causal link between the protected activity and the employer's action. (Yanowitz v. L'Oreal USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1042.) The FAC alleges protected activity. (FAC, pars. 7-8, 11 [plaintiff's complaints re FEHA and Labor Code violations].) The pleading sufficiently alleges a causal nexus and an adverse employment action. (FAC., par. 17.) The wrongful termination in violation of public policy claim is well pleaded. The alleged FEHA and Labor Code violations support the claim. The harassment claim is well-pleaded. (FAC, par. 8.) The court further notes that defendant cites outdated law. In 2019, The Legislature clarified harassment law and noted "[a] single incident of harassing conduct is sufficient to create a triable issue regarding the existence of a hostile work environment if the harassing conduct has unreasonably interfered with the plaintiff's work performance or created an intimidating, hostile, or offensive work environment." (Government Code section 12923(b); Beltran v. Hard Rock Hotel Licensing, Inc. (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 865, 878-880 [noting less demanding standard and inapplicability of former cases]; CACI 2524 [defining severe or pervasive]; Bailey v. San Francisco District Attorney's Office (2024) 16 Cal.5th 611, 629-630 [use of single racial epithet could constitute severe conduct to support harassment claim].) The failure to prevent discrimination and harassment claims are sufficiently alleged based on the above. The demurrer to the Labor Code section 98.6 claim is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff merely alleges that he complained to his employer. He does not allege "protected activity" as particularly defined under section 98.6, meaning a complaint . (See Grinzi v. San Diego Hospice Corp. (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 72, 87; Weingand v. Harland Financial Solutions, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2012) 2012 WL 3537035 at *6-7 [mere internal complaints insufficient to support Labor Code 98.6 claim].) (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) =(301/CVA)
